Elden Ring feels very distinct from the rest of the Souls games and their imitators and is incredibly successful at really selling a feeling of pure adventure and exploration, on top of having a top-notch art style and combat mechanics. It's not perfect, some bosses are grueling, the map is big enough that it can be a turn-off to some, and the side quests are maddeningly easy to screw up. But it's as close to my ideal version of this game as it can get without reading my mind and that's gotta count for something.

Elden Ring is genuinely one of the best games I’ve ever played.

It’s not perfect, some bosses are annoying and the questlines are basically impossible without looking stuff up, but the game is still insane. The map feels massive without being empty, every random cave or elevator can lead to something cool, and there’s always some weird weapon, boss, armor set, or hidden area to find.

The combat is hard but addicting. You can get destroyed for an hour, change your build a little, learn the fight, then suddenly win and feel like you actually earned it. That’s what makes the game so good. It doesn’t just hand you wins, it makes you lock in.

The build variety is probably my favorite part. You can run strength, bleed, magic, faith, dex, whatever cursed setup you want, and somehow it can still work if you build it right.

Honestly, I get why people call this game a masterpiece. It’s frustrating sometimes, but in the best way. One of those games where you think you’re only playing for 30 minutes and then somehow it’s 4 AM.

I've played Overwatch since the day it released on Xbox One and only briefly took a break around 2019. As with most of us it has always been a love hate relationship and you won't always like the decisions being made, but I can confidently say that in its current form, Overwatch is the best it has ever been. Gameplay and overall fun wise. 

They seem to be taking a lot more risks as of late and it has been paying off. With Stadium at first, perks, and an assortment of other changes such as hero release schedule and more. If you want just a solid fun experience this really cannot be topped when it comes to a hero shooter. 

My one huge negative is the way the game is monetized but I understand the game needs to make money in some way and skins just happen to be the one thing everyone can agree on for the most part. It does make us original players miss the way we could just play the game and unlock the skins we wanted for the characters we played. The level up system isn't as good as the original Overwatch and that's about my biggest gripe still. 

Not a huge fan of hero bans or map voting, I'd rather just maps be random as it takes less time getting into match without either of these. Also, I feel like you should have to learn how to counter characters you don't like playing against, not just flat out ban them, but to each their own. 

As I stated, this doesn't really take away from my enjoyment of the game and I feel like the team has done an outstanding job of righting the ship of what was the sinking Titanic of Overwatch 2.

So I have no problem ranking up my Damage and Tank roles, but the moment I play Support, I’m hard-stuck in Bronze. Matchmaking in this game is awful—Bronze is filled with smurfs, and every opposing team feels cracked. No matter how much healing and damage I put out as Support (and in most games I’m outperforming the other supports, with the least amount of deaths), I still end up with actual Bronze teammates going against smurfs.

I went from winning 27/30 Tank games and 23/30 Damage games to now winning 3/30 Support games. What is actually going on with matchmaking? Why can I get my DPS and Tank roles up to Plat, but be stuck in Bronze 3 as Support?

When I do win, it’s “expected,” so I only get 7% progression, but when I lose, it’s 10%? Blizzard really needs to stop relying solely on wins and instead consider actual performance and stats.

At this point, I’ll only be playing Tank and Damage. At least the matchmaking feels fair there, and I can actually make progress. Thanks for ruining my Support experience, Blizzard.

It is fun, however, the fact that EA is so insistent on kernel-level-anticheat makes me ultimately not want to recommend this. Considering the fact this type of software has so much access to the things you are doing on your computer, it is a privacy and security nightmare. There are numerous examples of kernel level software being exploited and used to compromise users. It is such an unnecessary risk to take when there are still cheaters and server-side or user-space anticheat options that exist. The fact they refuse to outright support Linux despite it's increasing popularity and decreasing popularity of Windows is something I feel that EA will eventually decide to walk back on. Developing better solutions that don't require such low-level access is doable, it's just that EA refuses to do so.

This game a syringe of dopamine that shoots directly into your cortex from minute 1. There is a level of complexity to this game that most arpgs can only dream of. The kind of complexity that makes you buy a second monitor so you can glance at your 7 google chrome tabs faster. This game will overwhelm you at first, and it will take at least 200 hours before you feel like you understand what's happening, but even those first 200 hours of confusion are an absolute blast; because this game is also deceptively simple. In the campaign, you'll be experimenting with the crafting system, getting excited for that one modifier on your new weapon thatll make you that much stronger, not knowing that the scaling will take you to power level of multiverse traveling eldritch entities. You can spend 500 hours on 1 character, amd still have more progress to make. There's just one more perfect modifer to fish for. One more piece of gear to push you that much more beyond the line of sanity. 

The level of complexity and depth in this game will turn away many, but if you love incredibly deep systems, and masterful arpg design, Path of Exile will consume you like nothing else youve ever experienced. 

It is recommended that you try it for free to start, and then if you really love it, pay the "box price" of getting the proper stash tabs to make your life easy in the end game. Shouldnt run you more than $50. Less than that even if you wait for the devs to put stash tabs on sale, which happens every two weeks or so.

Phasmophobia is a game that has always been broken and I believe the community always expected to be broken to some degree, but the newest character animations and customizations update has ruined it to a point that I don't believe it'll ever recover from. And, no, I don't see this as me being a doomer or whatever and thinking the game is ruined simply because I don't like the update. The devs have shown they don't know what they're doing all the way to a main dev in a now-deleted post saying everyone will get "used to the jank" that this update introduced. 

Ok, let's just ignore the animations jank for a second then and focus on what else this update broke:
Some maps don't work as well or at all, an example being Sunny Meadows Restricted which has somehow become unrestricted and now loads the full version of the map. How that gets broken in an update for animations and customizations is incredible to me.
Items have become far less unresponsive, an example being the various medias (photo/video/audio) working only half the time for me, especially so for tier 1 and tier 2.
And the game itself has become far more unplayable, an example being how sometimes I'm unable to select "next" after finishing a contract, meaning I don't get the money and xp rewards. This feels GREAT after an hour-long contract.

I'll finish this review by saying I think this update has made me realize how much of a cult the devs have managed to create for this game, not intentionally, but that's what they've done. They haven't used the millions of dollars they've made to hire professional people, no, they've continued to develop this nearly 6 year old game slowly and with talent that often doesn't know what they're doing outside of the amazing maps and ghost types they've added. The characters look worse now, the animations shouldn't be this bad, and the game shouldn't be suffering this much. 

And if one of the Kinetic Games fans finds this review, I'll say this (provided you've made it this far): don't bother wasting your energy typing a reply, you've already won. The company has made millions, they continue to get good sponsorships (like Alan Wake), and they have good merch sales. I gave them my money around 5 years ago, and there's no taking that back, not that I'd want to because I've enjoyed the game up to this point. I'll continue to play periodically, but it's clear they have no intentions of fixing what they've done to the game, so it's time I move on to the other games collecting dust in my library. 

Thank you for the amazing 5-ish years of ghost hunting, Kinetic Games, and I do mean that, but this is clearly the end of an era for the game. I will not change this review if the game does get fixed because I want it to serve as a timestamp, but I will add an "EDIT" or whatever to show I no longer feel the way this review says I do.

As of this recent update, I do not have anymore good faith in the devs for this game anymore.

As of late, Phasmophobia has been seeing a sort of decline. At first, updates added new ghost equipment, an entirely new progression system, and new ghosts to shake up the gameplay. It was nice, and made the game interesting enough for me to keep coming back to. However, updates became less frequent and less significant in size. Map-related updates (mostly reworks) few times a year, as well as new badges and content from seasonal events. Map reworks were met with a little bit of controversy, but nothing too bad. Furthermore, people have complained about the game struggling with a consistent design in the gameplay loop. Progression demanded a lot of grinding, yet the gameplay loop required you to take your time with the game. It led to this sort of strange inconsistency in the game.

Me personally, I thought it was fine. It was still a game worth playing with friends every now and then, and I was okay with doing some grinding every now and then.

The new update, however, is much bad.

Conceptually, I like it. Newer animations and player customization sounded like a great way to refresh the game. However, it's really bad. The game is full of way more bugs now and is unplayable. My friend and I crashed twice, at the same time, in both attempts we tried to play. The game is so jank and broken now, that you cannot ignore it.

Even ignoring that, the new animations and customization systems make the game worse. Animations add a delay to every item you use, and were not designed with current items in mind. With high FOV players, every item you hold is now tiny on your screen, making certain items harder and unfun to use. As for customization, prices make no sense. Most customization items costing $10,000 and Prestige shirts costing $5,000 is insane, feeling as if the devs either don't care about new players or didn't think this through.

Make no mistake, Kinetic Games is not a small dev team struggling to get by. They have made millions of dollars from this game, are pushing for all kinds of merch and products around this game (even a movie), and have a full-size dev team for this game. After learning this, I don't have faith in the devs to pick up the slack and bring this game the justice it deserves.

As of now, do not buy the game. Wait for a sale if you really want to get this game. Though, even if a new patch comes around, your money is better spent elsewhere on games where the devs do care about the game they're making.

Although the game itself has a solid concept and I enjoyed it for many hours, I have to conclude that Phasmophobia cannot be recommended.

While the concept of determining the ghost type via evidences and behaviour is enjoyable, especially in multiplayer, the outright terrible design decisions of the developers can neither be ignored nor excused in many ways.

The following aspects soured the game massively for me:

- Extremely slow update speed, then buggy updates
This has probably been mentioned in other reviews, but the developer, Kinetic games, releases updates and content on an incredibly slow pace. And when they release them, the new content often feels underwhelming. The latest and most irritating example are the new custom character models. This feature has been in work for years and when it came out, the players found barely any options to customise their characters, but a ton of new bugs. Speech recognition not working anymore, numerous graphical glitches and even broken game sessions. In my case, a hunting ghost was permanently stuck in the walls, softlocking the session. This never happened before the latest update, so the excuse of quality control being the reason for the slow update frequency is just hard to believe at this point. 
Another instance is the number of maps. Only about one new map and one "reskin" is just not enough.
There is even a public Trello site in which the new and planned features are listed, however, the given dates seem to be more wishful thinking than any reasonable forecast. The game came out almost six years ago and collected hundreds of millions of euros. Lack of experience or means cannot be taken as an excuse anymore.

- Willful negligence against the community wishes
Kinetic Games has a long history of outright ignoring player requests. This goes from minor design details to major game breaking issues. One minor thing would be the number of carriable items or the addition of a duffle bag to prevent the game from becoming a walking simulator, especially in single player games or on large maps, making them far less enjoyable. 
A medium issue is the incorrect detection of media. If you go on reddit, you will be overwhelmed with images that show how broken the central feature of collecting media is. For example, there are photos of a ghost not being recognised, or an obvious D.O.T.S. ghost being recognised as a duplicate of a ghost writing book coincidentially lying somewhere in the right camera angle. A simple mechanic to detect multiple "things of interest" and automatically selecting the most valuable non-duplicate would solve the issue, but the developer has simply decided to not do anything about it.
A major unfixed issue are trolls and player taking a game hostage. All it needs is one guy playing loud music, hiding or misusing items, being afk, refusing to enter the van or repeatedly aborting the leaving sequence to ruin the session for all other players. A dead simple solution would be the option to vote kick players or reports being actually useful. If I can come up with a solution within a few minutes, how comes that the entirety of Kinetic games cannot? As this is an online game with lots of random players, the trolls are a serious and frequent issue simply ignored by the developer.
They do not even do anything against cheaters openly advertising to boost other player's levels for money.

- Recycling of events
This is a less important yet still notable issue, the frequent events in the game are most often the same recycled events and simply a grindfest. The lates Alan Wake event is a notable exception but again so full of bugs that it is not enjoyable again. Kinetic Games just made the game so dark that you cannot see much on them without a flashlight. And if this affects me with max brightness set and a high quality monitor with increased contrast, the "average/casual" gamer will suffer even worse.

- Mediocre optimisation
For what it is, the game lacks optimisation. I have seen games with much higher graphic quality having a similar hunger for hardware as Phas, meaning that the game is in dire need of some performance improvements.

-Lack of mod support
Kinetic games has made it clear that they will take action against modders of the game. On one hand, this is laughable as they seemingly take little action against cheaters while there are other reports of wrongfully banned players. On the other hand, I am fully confident that the large community could bring this game to its full potential in short time. Instead, the players are stuck with a slow and sluggish developer ignoring feedback. The game could be greatly improved with minimal company resources, but the developer chose otherwise.

All in all I suspect that Kinetic Games has been surprised by the success of Phasmophobia and cannot handle that. It feels like they have no proper plan for the game and don't really know what they are doing. From what I have heard, they earned hundreds of millions of Euros with the game, yet cannot bring themselves to frequent updates, bug free features or even simple yet effective quality of life improvements. I find that the rumor that Phasmophobia was just developed as a quick cash grab that got surprised by its own success increasingly plausible, as this would explain the many "odd" decisions and actions of the developer.

So while the concept is solid, the way things are going is so fundamentally wrong that I can only recommend to not purchase it until the mentioned things have changed fundamentally. If the developer worked properly, I would have strongly recommended the game, but seeing it suffer from massive neglect and game breaking bugs for years cannot be overlooked. The latest update making so many things actually worse for the players was the straw that broke the camel's back.

I have been playing Final Fantasy XIV on and off for about 10 years, and I currently have over 2,080 hours in the game. Looking back, one of my only real regrets is that I didn’t commit to it sooner instead of constantly switching between this and other MMOs like World of Warcraft. Every time I stepped away, something eventually pulled me back.

The biggest reason is the story. Over the years I have become incredibly attached to the world, its characters, and the journey your character takes. Unlike many MMORPGs where the story feels like background filler between gameplay, Final Fantasy XIV treats its narrative like a full RPG. There have been more moments than I can count where I could feel emotions building during important story scenes. The writing manages to deliver moments of triumph, heartbreak, humor, and hope in ways that genuinely stay with you long after you log out.

Another thing that makes the game stand out is the job system. Instead of creating multiple characters to play different classes, you can do everything on one character. My Warrior of Light can swap between being a Paladin, White Mage, Viper, or dozens of other jobs simply by equipping a different weapon. It’s incredibly freeing because it means you’re never locked into a role. If your group needs a tank, you can tank. If they need a healer, you can heal. If you feel like dealing damage today instead, you can do that too.

This flexibility makes playing with friends incredibly easy thanks to the game’s level syncing system. If a friend is new to the game and running a low-level dungeon, I can join them regardless of my level. The game will sync my character down to the appropriate level so the dungeon still feels balanced. Alternatively, I can switch to one of my lower-level jobs and level it alongside them. It’s a system that keeps older content relevant and makes it easy for veteran players to help new ones without ruining the experience.

You can also run older content synced or unsynced. Synced lets you experience the dungeon exactly as it was designed when it originally released. Unsynced allows you to enter at your current level and absolutely destroy it, which is great for farming mounts, collecting gear appearances, or just revisiting old content for fun.

Speaking of gear appearances… there is something every Final Fantasy XIV player eventually learns:

The true endgame is glamour.

The game has an incredibly deep glamour system, which allows you to change the appearance of your gear to look however you want. This has created an entire fashion culture within the game. Players spend countless hours collecting outfits, mixing armor pieces, designing themed looks, and showing them off in cities. Entire communities exist just to share glamour designs, and it genuinely feels like a creative part of the game rather than a small cosmetic feature.

Another aspect that deserves a lot of praise is the music. The soundtrack in Final Fantasy XIV is nothing short of incredible. Composer Masayoshi Soken has created hundreds of tracks that range from epic orchestral battle themes to emotional piano pieces and even unexpected genres like rock and metal. Many boss fights are elevated to legendary status purely because of how powerful the music makes the encounter feel.

The boss fights and raids themselves are also some of the most memorable in the MMO genre. From large-scale alliance raids with dozens of players to intense high-end savage encounters, the game does a fantastic job of creating battles that feel both mechanically engaging and visually spectacular.

Something else that keeps the game welcoming is the community. In my experience, Final Fantasy XIV has one of the friendliest MMO communities I’ve ever encountered. New players are often welcomed and helped rather than criticized, and the game even has systems in place that reward experienced players for helping beginners through content.

It’s also worth mentioning the free trial, which is honestly one of the most generous in gaming. New players can experience a massive portion of the game—including multiple expansions—without paying anything. That alone says a lot about how confident the developers are in the experience they’ve built.

After spending a decade with this game and logging over two thousand hours, I can confidently say that Final Fantasy XIV is one of the most memorable gaming experiences I’ve ever had. It isn’t just an MMO you log into for daily tasks. It’s a world you grow with over time, a story you become emotionally invested in, and a community you become part of.

And even after all these years, I still find myself logging in, finding something new to do, and thinking:

"I’m still not done with this adventure."

There is a special kind of irony in working a 10-hour shift in quality testing and manufacturing, only to come home, boot up the PC, and immediately clock in for a second shift building a brutalist mega-factory. But that is exactly what Satisfactory does to you.

This isn't just a base-building game; it is a massive, beautiful logistics engine. The sense of scale as you push past Phase 3 and into Tiers 7 and 8 is insane. The game runs flawlessly on an RTX 4070 Ti, and visually, there is nothing quite like standing on the edge of a canyon looking down at a massive, perfectly symmetrical concrete dam you built from scratch.

The genius of the game is in the problem-solving and how much it rewards precision. If you hate belt spaghetti, you learn to build 8-meter maintenance subfloors to hide the chaos and route your truck networks perfectly above ground. When you finally nail a flawless math ratio—like clocking a miner to exactly 270/min so you don't have to underclock a manifold of six constructors—it hits a very specific, incredibly satisfying part of the brain.

The developers also respect your time by constantly improving the quality of life. Playing on the 1.2 experimental update and finally being able to link machines together with power is an absolute game-changer for keeping decentralized outposts looking clean.

Put on some Carpenter Brut or Master Boot Record, grab a coffee, and start optimizing. If you have the mindset for tweaking, balancing, and building massive self-sustaining loops, this easily dominates a 300+ game library.

10/10.

Summary:
I literally had to uninstall this masterpiece just so I could remember to play the other games in my Steam library. You will spend hours building a tiny, pristine factory, feel a massive surge of pride, and then immediately realize it is wildly inefficient garbage and you have to rebuild the entire thing from scratch. 10/10 would exploit an alien planet again.

🎮 Gameplay:
[ ] Just press W
[ ] Fun
[x] Goodbye social life
Opinion: Warning: Highly radioactive levels of addiction. This is essentially a second, full-time engineering job that absolutely refuses to pay you a salary, and yet it's the finest gaming experience out there. I had to permanently uninstall it because I sensed the dark, consuming danger of our toxic relationship—it wanted my soul, and I was completely ready to give it up. Make sure to touch grass before you buy this.

🎨 Graphics:
[x] Runs on a microwave
[ ] Respectable pixel art
[ ] Melted my GPU
Opinion: The visuals are actually stunning, yet by some dark magic of optimization, the absolute potato that I proudly call my PC ran it incredibly smoothly. You get to enjoy beautiful, high-fidelity alien landscapes right before you completely pave over them with concrete and smog, all without dropping frames.

🎵 Audio:
[x] Play while listening to a podcast
[ ] Gets the job done
[ ] Mozart is crying in the shower
Opinion: The factory ambient noises are decent and get the job done, but let's be real—this is the ultimate second-monitor game. Just leave the game volume on low, fire up your favorite Spotify playlist or a 4-hour podcast, and fully dissociate while you route your conveyor belts.

🐛 Bugs:
[ ] It's a feature
[x] Only the funny ones
[ ] Smooth as butter
Opinion: Extremely stable for the sheer amount of chaos happening on screen. I ran into a couple of very minor graphical glitches here and there, but absolutely zero game-breaking jank that would ruin my massive spaghetti factories or brick my save file.

💰 Price:
[ ] Sell a kidney
[ ] Wait for a discount
[x] Worth every penny
Opinion: Are you looking to ruin your sleep schedule and take on an uncompensated corporate grind for FICSIT Inc.? Here is the path. Go ahead and drop full price on it without hesitation—it is undeniably worth every single penny.
